event: Views of the Roman Forum and Saint Peter's Square
symbols: Roman Forum ruins — the fallen glory of the pagan empire upon whose ruins the Church was built
St. Peter's Basilica and Square — the center of Christendom, built over the tomb of the apostle Peter
obelisk in the piazza — the pagan monument baptized into Christian use, the conquest of the cross over empire
description: Two panoramic views stacked vertically. The top image shows the Roman Forum with ancient ruined columns, arches, and scattered stonework in the foreground, with medieval and Renaissance buildings on the hillside behind. The bottom image shows St. Peter's Square with the great basilica at center, flanked by Bernini's curved colonnades. An obelisk stands in the center of the piazza, and small figures of people and carriages populate the square. Both views are rendered in muted, atmospheric tones with careful architectural detail.
theological_description: These paired views of Rome juxtapose the pagan past and the Christian present of the Eternal City. The Roman Forum, once the political and religious heart of the empire that crucified Christ and persecuted his followers, now lies in ruins — a visual fulfillment of Christ's prophecy that 'not one stone will be left upon another' (Matt 24:2), applied broadly to the transience of worldly power. Below it, St. Peter's Basilica rises in triumph over the traditional burial site of the apostle Peter, to whom Christ said, 'You are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matt 16:18). The contrast between ruined forum and living basilica enacts the biblical principle that God brings down the mighty and exalts the humble (Luke 1:52). The obelisk, originally brought from Egypt by Caligula, now stands crowned with a relic of the True Cross — pagan power claimed for Christ.

scripture_refs.reason: On this rock I will build my church — St. Peter's built over Peter's tomb fulfills this promise
Not one stone left upon another — the Forum ruins as emblem of worldly power's transience
He has brought down the mighty from their thrones — Rome's empire fallen, the Church enduring
